lesson.name: God is Dead, Nihilism & the Übermensch - Lesson 2: Nihilism

meta.courseTitle: God is Dead, Nihilism & the Übermensch
meta.lessonObjective: Understand what remains once the religious ground falls away, and why.

tasks.chapterTitle: First Essay: Good and Evil, Good and Bad
Second Essay: Guilt and Bad Conscience

messages.content:
Lesson 2: Nihilism - Presentation25 Slides
Guiding Quote: Willing Nothingness
  • “… he will sooner will nothingness than not will at all.”
  • This is Nietzsche’s sharpest formulation of nihilism: the human being would rather pursue an empty, self-denying goal than have no goal at all.
  • This lesson traces how nihilism arises once the religious meaning of suffering collapses, and what is left standing afterwards.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: What does Nietzsche identify as the route along which European civilisation slides toward nihilism?
questions.explanation: Direct statement on p.8 links pity morality to nihilism once religious ground falls.

questions.question: What new demand arises once belief in morality totters?
options: Acceptance of pity as highest good
questions.explanation: p.9 states the value of these values must be called into question.

questions.question: According to the text, what might the good man represent after religious authority is removed?
options: A symptom of retrogression or a narcotic
questions.explanation: p.9-10 raises the possibility that the good man endangers future potential.

questions.question: What does Nietzsche see in the instincts of pity and self-sacrifice once their religious halo fades?
questions.explanation: p.8 calls these instincts the great danger and seduction to nothingness.

questions.question: What replaces the lost religious certainty according to the lesson objective?
options: Knowledge of the conditions under which moral values grew
questions.explanation: p.9 requires examination of origins and distortions after the ground falls away.

cards.front: What remains when the religious ground falls away?
cards.back: Morality stands exposed as possible symptom, mask, disease, or danger to human potentiality.

cards.front: What route leads civilisation toward European nihilism?
cards.back: The morality of pity spreads wider and wider until it produces exhaustion and the will turning against life.

cards.front: Why must the value of moral values now be questioned?
cards.back: Their worth was taken for granted only under religious authority; once removed, this worth becomes doubtful.

cards.front: What does Nietzsche call the instincts of pity after religious sanction disappears?
cards.back: The great danger of mankind and seduction to nothingness.

cards.front: What vista opens when one halts at the problem of pity morality?
cards.back: Every species of doubt, mistrust, and fear springs up and belief in all morality totters.

cards.front: What might the good man function as once religious ground is gone?
cards.back: A narcotic by which the present battens on the future, preventing maximum human power and splendour.

cards.front: What outcome does pity morality threaten besides nihilism?
cards.back: A European Buddhism marked by stability, exhaustion, and backward-gazing melancholy.

cards.front: What knowledge becomes necessary after religious authority withdraws?
cards.back: Knowledge of the conditions and circumstances out of which moral values grew and became distorted.

cards.front: What does the text name as the most sinister symptom of modern European civilisation?
cards.back: The morality of pity that spreads wider and wider.

cards.front: What task replaces lost religious certainty?
cards.back: A critique that treats morality as result, symptom, mask, disease, cause, remedy, or drug.

slides.title: Guiding Quote: Willing Nothingness
bullets: “… he will sooner will nothingness than not will at all.”
This is Nietzsche’s sharpest formulation of nihilism: the human being would rather pursue an empty, self-denying goal than have no goal at all.
This lesson traces how nihilism arises once the religious meaning of suffering collapses, and what is left standing afterwards.

slides.title: The Collapse of Religious Morality
bullets: When the religious ground falls away, the morality of pity reveals itself as the path toward European nihilism.
Nietzsche traces how this morality, once taken as sacred, now accelerates the slide into nothingness.
slides.notes: Directly from the pivotal realization in the preface.

slides.title: Pity Morality as Symptom
bullets: The morality of pity is identified as the most sinister symptom of modern European civilisation.
It signals the exhaustion that turns the will against life itself.
slides.notes: Sets the stage for nihilism as outcome.

slides.title: From Pity to Nihilism
bullets: Nietzsche asks whether the spread of pity morality leads civilisation toward a new Buddhism or outright nihilism.
The question marks the moment when inherited religious values lose their sustaining power.
slides.notes: Core passage linking pity to nihilism.

slides.title: The Value of Values Questioned
bullets: A new demand arises: the value of moral values must itself be called into question for the first time.
Knowledge of the conditions under which these values grew becomes necessary once religious authority withdraws.
slides.notes: The critical turn after the religious ground falls.

slides.title: Morality as Possible Danger
bullets: Suppose the good man harbours a symptom of retrogression rather than progress.
Morality itself might then stand as the danger of dangers to the maximum potentiality of the human species.
slides.notes: Concrete consequence when values lose their ground.

slides.title: The New Vista Opened
bullets: Halting at the problem of pity morality opens an immense vista of doubt and mistrust.
Belief in all morality begins to totter once its religious foundation is removed.
slides.notes: Describes the vertigo after the ground falls away.

slides.title: What Remains After the Fall
bullets: Once religious sanction disappears, morality stands exposed as result, symptom, mask, or disease.
No automatic higher value attaches to the good man or the un-egoistic instincts.
slides.notes: Direct answer to the lesson objective.

slides.title: The Seduction to Nothingness
bullets: In the instincts of pity and self-sacrifice Nietzsche sees the great danger of mankind.
These instincts appear as seduction to nothingness once their religious halo fades.
slides.notes: Explains the mechanism of nihilism.

slides.title: European Buddhism as Precursor
bullets: The morality of pity threatens to deliver a European form of Buddhism.
This outcome replaces the old religious ground with passive resignation.
slides.notes: Specific image of what comes after.

slides.title: The Will Turning Against Life
bullets: Pity morality announces the last illness through its mincing melancholy.
The will turns against life precisely when religious supports are withdrawn.
slides.notes: Active description of resulting nihilism.

slides.title: Stability and Exhaustion
bullets: The exaggerated modern esteem for pity brings stability and exhaustion that gazes backwards.
Such exhaustion replaces the forward drive once religious meaning collapses.
slides.notes: Consequence for human striving.

slides.title: The Critique Now Required
bullets: We need a critique of moral values because their worth was previously taken for granted.
Without religious authority, this critique becomes the only remaining task.
slides.notes: Method after the ground falls.

slides.title: Conditions and Circumstances
bullets: Knowledge must now examine the conditions out of which moral values grew and under which they became distorted.
Morality appears as cause, remedy, stimulant, fetter, or drug once its religious origin is questioned.
slides.notes: Concrete investigative demand.

slides.title: No Indisputable Fact
bullets: The value of these values was never an indisputable fact beyond all question.
The religious ground alone had shielded them from examination.
slides.notes: Why questioning becomes inevitable.

slides.title: The Good Man Reconsidered
bullets: No one had doubted that the good man possessed higher value for human progress and prosperity.
After the religious ground falls, this judgment itself must be weighed as possible poison or narcotic.
slides.notes: Reversal made possible by loss of ground.

slides.title: Present Batten on the Future
bullets: The good man might function as a narcotic by which the present battens on the future.
Comfortable morality then appears meaner than its opposite once religious justification vanishes.
slides.notes: Troubling possibility opened by nihilism.

slides.title: Maximum Potentiality at Risk
bullets: Morality could be saddled with guilt if the maximum potentiality of human power and splendour is never attained.
The religious ground had hidden this risk from view.
slides.notes: Highest stake of the lesson.

slides.title: The Route to Nothingness
bullets: Pity morality is the route along which civilisation slides toward nihilism.
What remains is the recognition that this route was prepared by the very values once held sacred.
slides.notes: Summary of the mechanism.

slides.title: New Demand After Collapse
bullets: The new demand voices itself only after belief in morality totters.
A knowledge of origins and distortions replaces the lost religious certainty.
slides.notes: What the thinker must do next.

slides.title: Mistrust That Digs Deeper
bullets: A more and more fundamental mistrust arises against the un-egoistic instincts.
This mistrust digs deeper precisely because religious authority no longer protects those instincts.
slides.notes: Psychological process after the fall.

slides.title: The End Announced
bullets: The morality of pity announces the beginning of the end, stability, and exhaustion.
Religious ground removed, the will turning against life stands exposed as the remaining reality.
slides.notes: Final concrete image.

slides.title: The Task of Revaluation
bullets: After the religious ground falls away, the task is to place the value of morality in question.
Only this critique can determine whether morality itself endangers the splendour of the human species.
slides.notes: Forward direction from nihilism.

slides.title: Quiz · Self-test
